food_name: Yam Rice (Teochew Braised Taro with Rice)
food_subgroup: White rice mixed with braised taro (yam), topped with braised pork, mushrooms, dried shrimp, and spring onion; served at Teochew porridge stalls

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Teochew yam rice. Closest parent would be a cooked rice dish with mixed ingredients (FOODON:00002678 — rice dish), but no exact match exists for this Teochew preparation.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dishes — mixed rice with meat and vegetables (closest parent; no specific Teochew yam rice code exists in FoodEx2 hierarchy)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Teochew braised taro rice. A000E (rice dishes) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Teochew yam rice as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of yam rice weighs approximately 300g, comprising braised taro mixed into white rice with braised pork, mushrooms, and spring onion.

nutrient_source.title: Yam Rice (Teochew) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore — Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database for Teochew Yam Rice. Values represent the composite dish as served at Singapore hawker stalls.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Rice and Taro (Colocasia esculenta) — University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed composite estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 65 is a weighted composite estimate: white rice GI ~72 (University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008, Am J Clin Nutr) and boiled taro GI ~56 (University of Sydney GI Database) at approximately 60:40 rice-to-taro ratio by weight. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 75g) / 100 = 48.75, rounded to 48.8. This is an analogous composite estimate; no direct GI measurement for Teochew yam rice exists in the University of Sydney database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 150mg per 100g (450mg per 300g plate) from braising sauce; total meal sodium can reach 1,000–1,500mg when combined with typical Teochew porridge side dishes
hypertension.guidance: Limit to one plate and avoid high-sodium side dishes such as braised egg, preserved radish, and salted vegetables to keep total meal sodium below 800mg; the potassium content from taro (180mg/100g) provides a modest counterbalancing benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load of 48.8 per 300g plate (GI ~65, 75g available carbohydrate per serving); large carbohydrate portion is the dominant concern despite taro modestly lowering GI versus plain white rice
type2_diabetes.guidance: Reduce portion to half plate (GL ~24.4) for T2DM management; increase the proportion of taro relative to rice where possible, avoid additional rice top-ups, and pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (1.3g/100g, 3.9g per plate) and sodium (450mg per plate); cholesterol 15mg/100g (45mg per plate); overall fat profile is not severely atherogenic but cumulative meal sodium from side dishes is a relevant CVD risk factor
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Choose smaller portions, request less braising sauce to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and avoid adding high-fat braised pork belly or lard-based toppings to keep the dish within a heart-healthy meal pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat; taro provides anti-inflammatory micronutrients including potassium, magnesium, and vitamin B6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Yam rice is not a high-concern dish for rheumatoid arthritis at standard portions; monitor total meal sodium if on NSAIDs, as high sodium intake may interact with medication-related fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yam Rice (Orh Png / 芋头饭) is a signature Teochew Chinese dish widely served at Singapore hawker centres and Teochew porridge stalls. It consists of fragrant white rice mixed with cubed braised taro (yam / Colocasia esculenta), cooked in a savoury soy-based braising sauce with dried shrimp, and topped with braised pork, mushrooms, and spring onion. The taro is soft and slightly starchy with a mild, earthy sweetness that contrasts with the savoury braising sauce. A standard hawker plate weighs approximately 300g and provides 480 kcal, 15g protein, 12g fat, and 75g carbohydrate. The inclusion of taro (GI ~56) lowers the overall glycemic index of the dish to approximately 65 compared to plain white rice (GI ~72), but the large carbohydrate portion creates a high glycemic load of ~48.8 per plate, making portion control essential for T2DM patients. Sodium is 450mg per plate from the braising sauce; total meal sodium at a Teochew porridge stall can reach 1,000–1,500mg when side dishes such as braised egg, preserved radish, and salted vegetables are included. Taro contributes potassium (180mg/100g), magnesium, vitamin B6, and dietary fibre, providing modest micronutrient benefits. The dish is dairy-free and culturally significant as the flagship item of Singapore's Teochew porridge dining tradition, where it is served alongside multiple small braised side dishes.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (exact match for Teochew Yam Rice). GI is an analogous composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database values for white rice and boiled taro, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present. Core macros are complete and plausible.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Teochew braised dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — no hydrogenated fats used in traditional preparation). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per HPB source — no significant vitamin D contributors in this dish). GI of 65 is a weighted composite analogous estimate (white rice GI ~72 at 60% weight, boiled taro GI ~56 at 40% weight) from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Teochew yam rice exists. Glycemic load per serving = (65 × 75) / 100 = 48.75, reported as 48.8. FoodEx2 code A000E used as closest defensible parent (rice dishes); no specific Teochew yam rice code exists. Hindi local name 'अरबी चावल' (arabi chawal — taro rice) is the closest natural Hindi equivalent used in Singapore's Hindi-speaking community context. Aliases self-check (q8): all strings present in aliases_by_language verified verbatim in flat aliases array — english: [Yam rice, taro rice, Teochew yam rice, Teochew braised yam rice, yam rice with braised pork, Teochew porridge yam rice]; chinese: [芋头饭, 芋頭飯, 芋头饭 (Yù Tóu Fàn)]; malay: [Nasi Keladi]; tamil: [கோலோக்காசியா சாதம், கோலோக்காசியா சாதம் (Kōlōkkāciyā Cātam)]; hindi: [अरबी चावल]; romanized: [Orh Png, orh png, yù tóu fàn, Kōlōkkāciyā Cātam] — all confirmed present in flat aliases.
